text.title: Essential Reports, Key Metrics, and Quality Score
text.summary: Which Google Ads reports to use, what each metric means, how to interpret Quality Score, and how to segment for deeper diagnosis.

list.item: Overview Dashboard — high-level performance snapshot
Campaigns tab — campaign-level metrics, budget pacing
Keywords tab — keyword performance, Quality Scores
Search Terms (Keywords → Search Terms) — actual queries triggering ads
Ads and Assets — RSA performance, ad strength
Auction Insights — competitive position, impression share vs. competitors
Impressions — reach potential; low = increase budget or broaden targeting
CTR — ad relevance; low = improve ad copy or tighten keyword targeting
Quality Score (1-10) — overall health; low = improve ads + landing pages + CTR
Conversion rate — landing page effectiveness; low = optimize landing page
CPA — cost efficiency; high = reduce bids, add negatives, improve quality
Impression Share — market capture fraction; low = increase budget or bids

rule.statement: Define CPA and ROAS targets before launching; optimize toward these metrics, not clicks or CTR.
rule.rationale: Clicks and CTR are proxies, not outcomes. An account with high CTR and no conversions is failing. Defining success metrics upfront prevents optimizing for vanity numbers.

section.title: Quality Score
section.p: Quality Score is a 1-10 per-keyword composite of three components:

list.item: Expected CTR — predicted click rate for this keyword
Ad relevance — keyword-to-ad copy match
Landing page experience — post-click user experience and relevance

rule.statement: Track Quality Score weekly per keyword; a QS drop predicts CPA spikes 1-2 weeks ahead and signals tighter ad group theming or landing page work is needed.
rule.rationale: Quality Score affects both ad rank and CPC. A declining QS means you pay more for the same position. Catching it weekly allows corrective action before costs spike significantly.

list.item: Device: mobile vs. desktop performance — adjust bids by device
Time: day-of-week and hour patterns — dayparting adjustments
Network: Search vs. Search Partners — often partners have worse CTR and CPA
Location: geographic performance — exclude or increase bids by region
Audience: audience layer performance — bid adjustments by audience

rule.statement: Segment by device and network before making any campaign-level conclusion; mobile and Search Partners often skew the aggregate and hide different performance drivers.
rule.rationale: A campaign with "bad" average CPA may have excellent desktop CPA and terrible mobile CPA. Aggregates mask the diagnosis. Device segmentation is the first split to run.
